headline: Gulf Strikes and a Slowing Strait Put Monday's Open on Edge
body_html:
Markets are bracing for a tense Monday open after a weekend of military escalation in the Persian Gulf. U.S. forces conducted strikes on Iranian missile systems near the Strait of Hormuz, Tehran responded with missile launches toward Qeshm Island, and tanker traffic through the strait slowed to a multi-week low. Oil surged more than 3% on the renewed hostilities, rippling through currency markets and sending Indian equities lower as the rupee touched a one-month trough against the dollar.

The geopolitical flare-up lands atop a Friday session that was already showing fissures beneath the surface. While Consumer and Industrials names closed in the green — each gaining roughly 1% or better — Software and Semiconductors each shed nearly half a percent, and individual software names took sharper blows: identity-security firm Okta fell 6.86% and cybersecurity specialist CrowdStrike dropped 5.66%. Breadth across our 396-name universe finished 225 advancers against 168 decliners, a modest positive tilt that masks the sector divergence.

At the open, watch energy-adjacent names and any issuer with meaningful Hormuz supply-chain exposure. Jet-fuel stocks across Europe are already running thin heading into the conflict, and President Trump's assertion that the strait remains open to commercial traffic did little to calm shipping-risk premium. Trade-talk headlines — India is reportedly pressing for improved terms in U.S. negotiations — add a secondary geopolitical layer worth monitoring through the session.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Drops to Multi-Week Low Amid U.S.-Iran Strikes
briefs.body: Shipping movement through the Strait of Hormuz fell to its slowest pace in several weeks after U.S. forces struck Iranian missile installations in the area and Iran responded with missile launches. The exchange raised the safety risk premium for commercial vessels transiting the world's most critical oil chokepoint, according to Reuters reporting.

briefs.headline: Oil Jumps More Than 3% as Military Escalation Threatens Gulf Shipments
briefs.body: Crude prices surged more than 3% over the weekend as fresh strikes between U.S. and Iranian forces stoked fears of sustained disruption to Gulf energy flows. The move pressured importing economies, with India's rupee sliding to a one-month low and Mumbai equities selling off as oil's advance widened trade-deficit concerns.

briefs.headline: Europe's Jet-Fuel Inventories Near Critical Levels as Mideast Tensions Flare
briefs.body: European jet-fuel stocks have fallen to less than one month of supply at current consumption rates, leaving the continent with little buffer as Gulf hostilities intensify. Any sustained disruption to Hormuz traffic could accelerate the drawdown, potentially lifting aviation-fuel costs and squeezing airline margins heading into peak summer travel demand.

briefs.headline: Software and Cybersecurity Names Lead Friday's Decliners
briefs.body: The software complex ended Friday's session as the worst-performing group in our universe, off nearly half a percent as a group, with individual names posting sharper losses. Okta fell 6.86%, CrowdStrike dropped 5.66%, MongoDB shed 5.73%, and Zscaler declined 5.34%, suggesting sector-specific selling pressure beyond the broader geopolitical noise.

briefs.headline: Timber and Wood-Products Names Topped Friday's Leaderboard
briefs.body: Boise Cascade surged 5.29% and Weyerhaeuser gained 4.22% on Friday, making timber and wood-products the standout pocket of strength in an otherwise mixed session. Consumer-facing names Crocs and Abercrombie & Fitch also posted gains above 4%, reflecting resilient demand sentiment in footwear and apparel heading into the new week.

briefs.headline: India Digs In on U.S. Trade Terms as Geopolitical Costs Mount
briefs.body: New Delhi is holding out for more favorable conditions in its ongoing trade discussions with Washington, signaling that negotiations remain unresolved despite broader diplomatic pressures. The stance complicates the bilateral agenda at a moment when rising oil prices — driven by Hormuz uncertainty — are already straining India's external accounts.
